name: Upstash Serverless Redis
what_it_does: Provides a fully managed serverless Redis database accessible over HTTP/REST with per-request pricing and zero idle cost.
use_cases: Cache LLM responses or expensive API call results to reduce latency and cost in agent pipelines
Implement distributed rate limiting across multiple agent instances using atomic Redis commands
Store and retrieve short-lived session state or conversation history between stateless agent invocations
Use sorted sets to maintain priority queues for agent task scheduling across serverless functions
Pub/sub messaging between coordinating agent workers using Redis channels over HTTP
not_for: Primary persistent data storage requiring durability guarantees beyond Redis AOF/RDB
Large binary blob storage — Redis is optimized for small structured values, not file storage
Applications needing Redis Cluster with multi-shard horizontal scaling (Upstash uses single-region sharding)
best_when: You need a low-latency cache or ephemeral store callable from serverless functions or edge runtimes where a persistent TCP Redis connection is not viable.
avoid_when: Your workload requires very high sustained throughput (millions of ops/sec) at low cost, where a dedicated Redis cluster would be more economical.

auth.notes: Each database has a unique REST URL and token. Token is passed as a Bearer token in the Authorization header or as a query parameter. Read-only tokens are available for scoped access.

pricing.paid_tiers: Pay-as-you-go at $0.2 per 100K commands; Pro plans from $280/month for dedicated databases with higher throughput
pricing.notes: No idle cost — you only pay per command executed. Free tier resets daily. Bandwidth charges apply above free limits.

performance.rate_limits: Free tier: 10K commands/day; Pay-as-you-go: 1000 requests/second per database by default; higher limits on Pro

agent_readiness.error_message_notes: HTTP status codes are standard and well-documented; Redis-level errors are surfaced in the response body with descriptive messages. Rate limit errors include Retry-After headers.
agent_readiness.idempotency_notes: Redis commands like SET with NX flag are inherently idempotent; general commands are not — agents must design for idempotency at the application layer.
known_agent_gotchas: HTTP overhead adds 2-10ms vs raw TCP Redis — avoid using it for ultra-low-latency tight loops
Default TTL is none — agents must explicitly set EXPIRE or keys persist indefinitely and accumulate storage cost
Pipeline batching (multiple commands in one HTTP request) is supported but requires specific REST payload format that differs from standard Redis client usage
Rate limit is per database, not per key — one runaway agent loop can exhaust the daily free quota for all agents sharing the database
The REST API does not support all Redis commands — SUBSCRIBE/PSUBSCRIBE for real-time pub/sub are not available; use Upstash QStash or polling patterns instead

security_score_components.security_notes: TLS enforced on all connections. Read-only tokens provide some scope separation. No fine-grained per-key ACLs as in self-hosted Redis ACL system. Token rotation requires manual database token regeneration.
